coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots doped with silver as lubricating oil additive for enhancing tribological performance at various temperatures
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Junhai

Deng, Yuanyuan
Chen, Shihao
Liang, Wenfeng
Zhang, Lixiu
Wei, Xiaoyi
Gao, Siyang
Wan, Ye
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doped with silver (Ag-CQDs) was prepared using a facile hydrothermal method. With the combination of ultrasound vibration and ionic liquid (IL), the dispersion stability of the Ag-CQDs additive in poly alpha olefin (PAO) was improved. The tribological performance of the CQDs and Ag-CQDs as additives over a wide temperature range were evaluated through four-ball tests and a ball-on-disk reciprocating configuration. The four-ball test results revealed that the CQDs and Ag-CQDs additives could effectively reduce the coefficient of friction (COF) of the base oil and wear scar diameter (WSD) of lower balls. When the concentration was 0.05 wt%, the Ag-CQDs additive could lead to 42.2% lower COF and 35.3% lower WSD than the base oil. Meanwhile, the tribological performance at elevated temperatures results revealed that the CQDs and Ag-CQDs additives could substantially decrease friction and wear when the ambient temperature was above 200 °C. The Ag-CQDs exhibited better lubricating performance than the CQDs. The lubricating mechanism was attributed to the formation of a tribofilm composed of Ag-CQDs, multicomponent oxides, carbonates and nitrides induced by tribochemical reactions, which provided effective protection for the rubbing pair and maintained low friction and wear.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots doped with silver as lubricating oil additive for enhancing tribological performance at various temperatures

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doped with silver (Ag-CQDs) was prepared using a facile hydrothermal method. With the combination of ultrasound vibration and ionic liquid (IL), the dispersion stability of the Ag-CQDs additive in poly alpha olefin (PAO) was improved. The tribological performance of the CQDs and Ag-CQDs as additives over a wide temperature range were evaluated through four-ball tests and a ball-on-disk reciprocating configuration. The four-ball test results revealed that the CQDs and Ag-CQDs additives could effectively reduce the coefficient of friction (COF) of the base oil and wear scar diameter (WSD) of lower balls. When the concentration was 0.05 wt%, the Ag-CQDs additive could lead to 42.2% lower COF and 35.3% lower WSD than the base oil. Meanwhile, the tribological performance at elevated temperatures results revealed that the CQDs and Ag-CQDs additives could substantially decrease friction and wear when the ambient temperature was above 200 °C. The Ag-CQDs exhibited better lubricating performance than the CQDs. The lubricating mechanism was attributed to the formation of a tribofilm composed of Ag-CQDs, multicomponent oxides, carbonates and nitrides induced by tribochemical reactions, which provided effective protection for the rubbing pair and maintained low friction and wear.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy conservation has been one of the most concerned issues in the scientific community and considerable challenges remain [1-4]. The friction and wear in modern industry account for about 23% of the world’s total energy dissipation [5]. In addition to energy waste and environmental pollution, friction and wear also cause failure of components in moving mechanical systems. Therefore, tribological optimizations and improvements, such as friction and wear reduction, are increasingly thought of as a key strategy for energy conservation. Currently, lubricants are the most effective approach to controlling friction and wear and thereby extending the service life of the mechanical systems and cutting down the costs of production. The lubricating oil is a substance introduced between the moving mechanical parts to serve the purposes of minimizing friction and wear, dissipating heat, eliminating and sealing wear debris, and preventing corrosion or rust. All lubricating oils contain additives, which are substances used to enhance the capacity of the base stock [6-8]. The modern industry requires that the moving parts should operate under harsh conditions, which points to the need for further tribological research efforts on developing low-friction efficient lubricating additives.

Due to their small size, surface effect, high carrying capacity, and excellent chemical stability, nanoadditives can effectively protect the friction pair against severe wear by improving the contact state and forming a protective layer. Carbon-based nanomaterials, including fullerene, graphene, carbon nanotubes, nanodiamond, and their derivatives, have been widely used as solid lubricants and oil additives due to their superior physicochemical and mechanical properties as well as their unique self-lubricating capacity [9-15]. CQDs have opened up a new kind of carbon nanomaterial and have attracted increasing attention since they were discovered in 2004 [16]. Characterized by an ultrasmall size, tunable surface functional groups, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, and excellent dispersibility and stability, CQDs have been recognized by the tribology community as an energy-saving, high-precision lubricating material that can help to extend the service life of mechanical systems [17-20]. Meanwhile, a number of synthesis methods, including arc discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical approach, acid oxidation, hydrothermal/solvothermal, pyrolysis, microwave, and ultrasonic, and low-cost precursors (including milk, orange juice, amino acids, and carbon-based materials), have been developed and used to fabricate this nanomaterial [21]. Inspired by the advancement in CQDs synthesis, researchers have attempted to improve the tribological properties of CQDs and make this material suited for specific applications by designing and controlling its carbon core and surface groups. Typical strategies for achieving the aforementioned goals are surface modification and heteroatom doping. Ye et al. prepared CQDs doped with nitrogen by the pyrolysis method from critical acid and 4-Amipdiphenylamine. Friction tests revealed that the added nitrogen could enhance the thermal stability and film-forming capacity of CQDs [22]. CQDs with nitrogen-containing functional groups could be rapidly adsorbed on the worn surface with positive charge, resulting in low friction. Zhang et al. synthesized boron/nitrogen co-doped CQDs (B/N-CQDs) by a facile hydrothermal method. They found that the B/N-CQDs could be used as an additive to significantly improve the lubricating performance of polyethylene glycol (PEG) [23]. Zhao et al. prepared F doped CQDs as a lubricating additive. They found that the added fluorine could contribute to the layer formation of CQDs, and experience tribochemical reaction to produce ferric fluoride, which could repair worn surfaces [24]. Generally, doped elements have much higher reactivity toward the contact surface than traditional carbon and oxygen elements. This advantage makes it much easier for doped elements to form a practical tribochemical reaction film to alleviate the friction and wear of the rubbing pair [25-28]. In brief, the functional heteroatom doping strategy can endow CQDs with specific compositions, structures and surface chemistries that eventually enhance their lubricating performance.

Metal and Metal oxides are commonly employed to strengthen the mechanical and thermal properties of the composite lubricants as a strategy owing to their thermal stability, chemical inertness, good mechanical property and outstanding lubrication performance[29,30]. Among diverse metal elements, Ag component has been proved to be a potential functional candidate for antiwear and lubrication effects[31,32]. In Zhou’s research, the added Ag component could enhance the thermal and mechanical properties of the epoxy resin composite, and play a role in lubrication[33]. Wang et al. have found that the Ag nanospheres enhanced the lubricating effect of the graphene-based composite, which could be attributed to that the spherical morphology of Ag particles could convert sliding friction into rolling friction[34]. Meng et al. have deposited on the surface of the MWCNT as oil additive, and found that Ag/MWCNT contributed to minimizing friction and wear by filling valleys and grooves and forming physical deposition film on the worn surface [35]. As a consequence, doping silver component is a feasible strategy for enhancing the thermal and lubricating properties of the CQDs.

Although CQDs show good potential to minimize friction as a lubricating additive, their long-term dispersion stability in the base fluid is not sufficiently high, which limits their application. The CQDs tend to generate aggregates via the irreversible clustering of individual dots, forming bigger structures that deprive them of their ultrasmall size property and even cause them to undergo sedimentation. This tendency stems largely from the surface chemistry, the characteristics of the solvent, and the concentration of the CQDs additive. Ionic liquids (ILs) are salts of organic cations and inorganic or organic anions with a melting point below 100 °C. ILs have a great deal of advantageous physiochemical characteristics, including non-volatility, non-flammability, excellent thermo-oxidative resistance, and dipolar structure [36,37]. Recently, ILs have been accepted as a stabilizer and medium for synthesizing and modifying carbon-based nanomaterials. Wang et al. synthesized an imidazolium-based IL-caped carbon dots additive, which displayed excellent dispersion stability and enabled significantly improved tribological performance of PEG [38]. Shang et al. prepared CQDs-IL as a lubricating oil additive, which allowed for enhancing dispersion in PEG without any prominent precipitation as well as improving tribological properties of the base oil [39]. Mou et al. utilized IL-modified CQDs to realize enhanced suspension in PEG and improved lubricating properties of the base stock [40]. The research found that the PEG suspension could achieve optimal lubricating performance under different test conditions when added with 1.5 wt% of the CQDs-IL additive. Ma et al. fabricated IL-modified CQDs nanoparticles as an oil additive to improve the dispersion stability, and obtained a super-low friction coefficient and wear rate with an additive concentration of 3.6 wt% [41]. Consequently, functional IL-modified CQDs show great potential as an efficient, eco-friendly, and dispersion-promoting nanoadditive [42].

Here in this paper, using a facile hydrothermal synthesis method, a functional Ag-CQDs compound as an oil additive was prepared from critical acid, ethylenediamine, and silver acetate for the first time. The 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate (IL) was utilized along with ultrasonic treatment to endow the prepared Ag-CQDs additive with better dispersion stability in the PAO base oil. The oil suspensions of Ag-CQDs were applied to metal/metal or ceramic/metal tribo-pairs for reducing the friction and wear under different frictional conditions. The XRD, FTIR, TEM, SEM-EDS, TGA, Raman and XPS characterizations were conducted to determine the chemical nature, structure, and morphology of the Ag-CQDs compound and the states of the worn surface after friction tests. The lubricating mechanism of the Ag-CQDs additive was reasonably proposed based on the characterization analysis as well as the friction test results.

The citric acid (CA) and silver acetate were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The commercially available ethylenediamine was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate (IL) was supplied by Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Lanzhou, China). The poly alpha olefins type 6 (PAO6) was bought from Shenyang Hongcheng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China). All these chemicals were used as received without any further treatment.

The detailed preparation procedure was as follows: 1.5 g of CA, 0.2 g of sliver acetate, 1.5 mL of ethylenediamine and 20 mL of distilled water were mixed and stirred with a glass rod until a transparent solution was generated. Then, the solution was transferred to a reaction kettle and reacted for 8 h at 180 °C. When the reaction was complete, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to obtain a supernatant. The supernatant was then filtered with 0.22 μm membrane filtration, and was dialyzed for 24 h (the water was changed every 3 h) using a 500 Da dialysis bag to remove the small molecular impurities. The solvent was placed in a freeze dryer for 24 h to obtain some high-quality Ag-CQDs. A schematic illustration of the Ag-CQDs synthesis process was presented in Fig. 1 . Using the same process, 1.5 g of critic, 1.2 mL of ethylenediamine and 20 mL of distilled water were mixed to obtain some high-quality CQDs for comparison.

PAO6, a kind of commercially available synthetic aviation oil, was selected as the base fluid because of its high viscosity index, good thermal stability, high flash point, low pour point, and environmental friendliness. The basic physicochemical index parameters of PAO6 included: density of 0.83 g/cm3, flash point of approximately 230 °C, pour point of approximately −55 °C, viscosity index of 135, volatility of 6.1%, and viscosity of 32.5 mm2/s at 40 °C and 3.9 mm2/s at 100 °C. Additionally, the introduction of the 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate produced a steric hindrance effect between the nanomaterials, which resulted in increased dispersion stability of the additive in the base oil. Certain amounts (0.0005 g/0.0015 g/0.0025 g/0.004 g/0.005 g) of Ag-CQDs were added into 10 μL of IL in an agate mortar, and then melded with a pestle in a continuous mill for 30 min to obtain a heterogeneous blend. Subsequently, the blend was added into 5 g of PAO oil to obtain oil mixtures of certain concentrations (0.01 wt% / 0.03 wt% / 0.05 wt% / 0.08 wt% / 0.1 wt%). Finally, all the oil mixtures were continuously sonicated in an ultrasonic bath at 40 kHz of frequency for 2 h to obtain homogeneous dispersions. The resulting oil mixtures were stood for a few days to undergo dispersion evaluation and tribological investigation.

The XRD7000 X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Shimadzu, Japan) with an alterable slit width was utilized to produce X-ray diffraction patterns, and the data was assembled at a scanning speed of 5°/min from 2θ = 10° to 90° with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the prepared nanoparticles were obtained on a FEI Talos F200s transmission electron microscope (Thermo Scientific, USA) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Size distribution analysis was performed on the CQDs and Ag-CQDs compounds utilizing the Zetasizer Lab particle size analyzer (LPSA, Malvern Panalytical, England). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using the STA 449 F5 thermal analyzer (NETZSCH, Germany). The TGA experiment was carried out in Ar atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C/min and across a temperature range from room temperature to up to 700 °C. The functional group compositions of the CQDs and Ag-CQDs were detected with the Nocole iS5 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Scientific, USA), and the measurements were obtained across a range from 500 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1.

Using the MMW-1 friction tester, the four-ball tests were conducted to characterize the tribological performance of CQDs and Ag-CQDs as oil additives under room temperature conditions. The lubricating property of PAO oil with IL alone (5 g of PAO with 10 μL of IL) was also performed under the same conditions for comparison. The frictional pairs were GCr15 bearing steel balls (chemical composition: ≤0.1 wt% Mo, ≤0.3 wt% Ni, ≤0.25 wt% Cu, 0.95–1.05 wt% C, 0.15–0.35 wt% Si, 0.25–0.45 wt% Mn, 1.40–1.65 wt% Cr and balance Fe) that were 12.7 mm in diameter and 62 ∼ 64 HRC in hardness. In the process of the test, the contact interfaces of the upper ball and lower ball were immersed in the oil sample, and the friction coefficients were automatically recorded by the attached software. The test was conducted at room temperature with a load of 392 N and a rotation speed of 1450 rpm, lasting for 30 min. Five measurements were performed for each test, and the mean COF and WSD were reported, with error bars provided to understand the repeatability of the results. In addition, the maximum nonseizure load (PB value) of different oil mixtures was obtained at room temperature at a rotating speed of 1450 rpm for 10 s according to the ASTM D2783 standard. The VHX 1000 digital super-depth microscope (Keyence, Japan) was used to measure the WSDs of lower balls. The S4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi, Japan) was used to observe the morphology of the wear scars of lower balls. The surface roughness of the wear scar was measured by using S3C (Taylor Hobson, UK) roughness tester.

The tribological performance of the PAO base oil, PAO with the CQDs dispersion and the Ag-CQDs dispersion when used in ceramic/metal contacts across a wide temperature range were evaluated on the MFT 5000 multifunctional tribological tester (Rtec, USA) with a ball-on-disk configuration. Friction tests were carried out by pressing an upper fixed Si3N4 ceramic ball (6.35 mm in diameter and around 2000 HV in hardness) over a lower running GH4169 superalloy disk (composition: ≤0.08% C, ≤1.0% Co, 2.8–3.3% Mo, 0.3–0.7% Al, 0.75–1.15% Ti, 17–21% Cr, 50–55% Ni and balance Fe, at weight fraction；20 mm × 20 mm × 3 mm in size and around 85 ∼ 87 HRC in hardness) under the reciprocating sliding mode. The ball and disk specimens were cleaned by acetone in an ultrasonic bath for 15 min prior to each friction test. During the test, the applied nominal load was 15 N, and the reciprocating frequency was 2 Hz with an amplitude of 10 mm. The tests, each lasting for 15 min, were carried out at four different ambient temperatures: 25 °C, 100 °C, 200 °C, and 300 °C. The instant COFs were recorded by the attached software during the test, and three replicated surveys were taken for each friction test to acquire convincing COFs with error bars. Before the friction test, the 5 mL of oil mixture was added into the oil bath to guarantee that the contact was immersed in the oil. Furthermore, in order to compensate the evaporated oil, another 3 mL of oil mixture was added when the test temperature reached the set point. After the friction tests were complete, the S4800 SEM, which was integrated with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS), was employed to observe the worn surfaces of the superalloys. The 3D morphology and 2D profile of the alloy plates after friction tests were measured on the Rtec Ups-sigma profilometer (Rtec, USA). The morphology of upper Si3N4 ball was observed by the VHE-1000 super-depth optical microscope. An ultrasmall tribofilm was extracted from the worn surface of the alloy using the focused ion beam (FIB) technology. This tribofilm was then characterized via TEM coupled with EDX. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, which was intended to reveal potential tribochemical reactions that occurred on the worn surface of the superalloys after sliding tests, was performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB250, TA, USA) with Al Kα X-ray source. The test energy was 1486.8 eV, and the voltage of test tube was 15 kV with the current of 10 mA. The background vacuum of the analysis chamber was 6 × 10-8 mbar. Additionally, all the binding energies were adjusted using the C1s peak at 284.6 eV as the reference. The test samples were subjected to Raman spectroscopy using a laser confocal Raman spectrometer (XploRA PLUS, HORIBA, France) with an excitation wavelength of 638 nm.

The TEM images and size distributions of CQDs and Ag-CQDs were displayed in Fig. 2 , confirming the synthesis of CQDs and Ag-CQDs with spherical morphologies with mean diameters of approximately 3.51 nm and 3.57 nm, respectively. A lattice spacing of 0.34 nm corresponding to the (002) lattice plane of graphite was observed in the HR-TEM image [43]. The LPSA results revealed that the prepared CQDs and Ag-CQDs have narrow size distributions ranging from 2.12 nm ∼ 4.85 nm and 2.17 nm ∼ 4.96 nm, respectively. Fig. 3 a reveals the XRD patterns of the CQDs and Ag-CQDs powders. For Ag-CQDs nanoparticles, a broad peak was observed at 21.5°, which was attributed to the highly disordered carbon atom and the amorphous phase [44]. Other diffraction peaks were attributed to the silver-doped ingredient (PDF87-0720). The FTIR spectrums of CQDs and Ag-CQDs were illustrated in Fig. 3b. In the FTIR spectrum, typical bands which represented the stretching vibrations of O-H/N-H groups at around 3450 cm−1 could be observed [45]. The peaks at 1720 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibrations of C = O/C = C groups. The N-H bending deformation vibration (1556 cm−1), C-N vibration (1402 cm−1), and C-H skeletal vibration (1195 cm−1) were also detected [46]. Moreover, the peaks at 2932 cm−1 and 2861 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of the –CH3 band for Ag-CQDs nanoparticles, and the peak around 663 cm−1 could be ascribed to the Ag-N band, suggesting that coordination had occurred between silver and nitrogen. The TGA was utilized to measure the thermal stability and relative mass fraction of CQDs and Ag-CQDs. As indicated by the TGA curves, the two samples appeared to have similar degradation profiles, and Ag-CQDs began to experience sharp weight loss from a temperature of 351 °C, as opposed to CQDs from a lower temperature of 305 °C. This suggested that the doping silver would improve the thermal stability of carbon dots. The thermal residue fractions of CQDs and Ag-CQDs appeared to be 4.9 wt% and 11.5 wt%, respectively. These results showed that Ag-CQDs had better thermal stability, which was associated with the introduction of the silver component.

The excellent dispersion stability of nanoparticles in the base fluid was clearly crucial for their applications as lubricating additives [47,48]. The still standing observation method was employed to investigate the suspension stability of the CQDs and Ag-CQDs additives in the PAO base oil under ultrasonic treatment with the assistance of IL. According to Fig. 4 a, the CQDs and Ag-CQDs additives exhibited good dispersion stability in base oil after IL medication and ultrasonic treatment. No precipitates of agglomerates were observed at the bottom or on the wall of the bottle, even when the suspension was standing for 30 days, suggesting that the chemical IL modification and physical ultrasonic treatment enhanced the suspension capacity of nanoadditives in PAO. Fig. 4b displays the Zeta potential of the different samples and potential dispersion mechanism. The abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl of polar functional groups around the CQDs and Ag-CQDs would generate hydrogen bonding interaction with the IL, enabling the formation of a double electric layer between the nanoparticles. This, along with the repulsive force between the nanoparticles, might endow the carbon dots with better dispersibility and stability in the oil, thus alleviating the aggregation of the nanomaterials. Meanwhile, the long-chain alkane structure in the IL produced favorable compatibility between the selected IL and PAO molecules, which caused the carbon dots modified with the IL to suspend in the base oil. The Zeta potential values could be employed to evaluate the nanoadditives' dispersion stability in the base liquid. Under the same conditions, the greater the Zeta potential (absolute value) was, the more stability the nano-suspension system could have [49]. It could be observed from Fig. 4b that the added IL could effectively boost the absolute Zeta potential values of the carbon dots and thereby improve the nanoadditives’ dispersion stability. Meanwhile, the modified CQDs/IL and Ag-CQDs/IL had negative Zeta potentials, suggesting that the additives were expected to be adsorbed on the metal surfaces during the friction tests.

The friction and wear performance of PAO, PAO with IL alone, PAO containing 0.05 wt% CQDs/IL additive, and PAO containing 0.05 wt% Ag-CQDs/IL additive under four-ball test conditions were shown in Fig. 5 . As shown in Fig. 5a, the COF curve of the base oil was fluctuant with a relatively high COF value of around 0.116. The friction efficient value of PAO with IL alone was close to that of PAO oil, which suggested the tiny amounts of IL did not influence the lubricating property of base oil. Clearly, the use of a small amount (0.05 wt%) of CQDs and Ag-CQDs as additives contributed to dramatical reducing COFs of the PAO base oil. The CQDs dispersion and Ag-CQDs dispersion had COFs of 0.076 and 0.067, which were 34.1% and 42.2% lower than that of the base oil. Fig. 5b shows the mean COFs and WSDs of the lower balls lubricated by various lubricating samples. It could be observed that the CQDs and Ag-CQDs could improve the base PAO oil’s load-carrying capacity as well as its antiwear performance. Compared with that of PAO (662 μm), the WSD values of the CQDs and Ag-CQDs dispersions were reduced by 29.8% (465 μm) and 35.3% (428 μm), respectively. It was well known that the maximum nonseizure load (PB value) reflected the load-carrying capacity of the lubricating medium. The Table 1 presents the PB values of different lubricating samples under the four-ball test condition. The PB value of the base oil was about 490 N, which has the lowest load-carrying capacity among all the test samples. The PB value of PAO with IL alone was similar with the base oil, and this illustrated that the added IL did not obviously improve the load-carrying capacity of base oil. The CQDs and Ag-CQDs were able to effectively promote the load-carrying capacity of base oil, and the PAO with the Ag-CQDs additive had the highest PB value (710 N) compared with the rest of the samples.

To better illustrate the lubricating mechanism of the carbon dots additives for the steel/steel rubbing pair under four-ball test conditions, the SEM morphologies of the wear scars of the lower steel balls lubricated by different lubricating mediums under 392 N are presented in Fig. 6 . As shown in Fig. 6a, in the case of lubrication by PAO alone, wide and deep furrows with plenty of wear debris were present on the wear scars. Moreover, some severe scuffs were also observed, and the surface roughness of Ra was around 3.864 μm. These suggested that direct contact might have occurred between the rubbing pair in partial areas. The morphology of the wear scar lubricated by PAO with IL alone was similar with that of the PAO base oil (Fig. 6b). In contrast, in the case of lubrication by the suspensions with 0.05 wt% CQDs and 0.05 wt% Ag-CQDs, the wear scars appeared relatively smooth without obvious scuffs (Fig. 6c and d). Moreover, the furrows were relatively shallow and narrow, and the surface roughness values were smaller compared with that of base oil and PAO containing IL alone. These suggested that the introduction of CQDs and Ag-CQDs played a remarkable role in enhancing the antiwear performance of the base stock and protecting the contact interface between the rubbing pair. In general, positive charge would occur on the worn surface due to low-energy electrons emitted during friction [38]. Meanwhile, the carbon dots additives were adsorbed around the positive charge on the worn surface as a result of the abundant oxygen/nitrogen surface functional groups of nanoadditives. Moreover, the added additives could quickly enter the asperity of the wear scar for repair due to the small particle size [50]. Consequently, the CQDs and Ag-CQDs additives effectively alleviated direct contact between the rubbing pair, resulting in low COFs of the test stage.

The COF curves and mean COFs of PAO, PAO containing 0.05 wt% CQD/IL and PAO containing 0.05 wt% Ag-CQDs/IL at various temperatures were presented in Fig. 7 . It was clear that the PAO base oil had low COFs values of 0.125 and 0.190 at 25 °C and 100 °C, respectively. This indicated that the synthetic oil had excellent lubricating performance at relatively low temperatures. However, the COF curve was not stable and had an obvious fluctuation at 200 °C, and the mean COF value increased to 0.351. The reason for this might be that the viscosity of the oil became several times lower at high temperatures, resulting in breaks in the oil film on the contacts between the rubbing pair. The mean COF reached its maximum of 0.479 throughout the whole test when the temperature was 300 °C. This suggested that the base oil had lost its lubricating nature at such temperature. Compared with pure oil, the CQDs/IL dispersion and Ag-CQDs/IL dispersion had lower COF values at 25 °C and 100 °C, and the COF curves remained stable almost without fluctuation during the friction tests. This suggested that the added additive was effective in improving the lubricating capacity of the base oil at comparatively low temperatures in a reciprocating friction configuration. Due to their ultrasmall size, the CQDs and Ag-CQDs compounds could easily go into the contact surface, which caused the contact surface to experience repair and low shear force. In particular, the added silver component could enhance the load capacity of the oil film, resulting in the lowest COF values at a relatively low temperature range. Above 200 °C, the COF values of the oils containing the CQDs and Ag-CQDs additives were significantly decreased compared with those of the pure oil. This suggested that the CQDs and Ag-CQDs additives played a major role in lubrication when the oil oxidized. Some of the friction-induced heat could be taken away by the carbon materials, and this might, to a moderate extent, slow the temperature rise of the contact surface. Moreover, the ultrasmall carbon dot additive, which served as a solid lubricant, could cause the formation of a tribofilm at elevated temperatures over the course of friction. As a consequence, the COFs of base oils containing the CQDs and Ag-CQDs additives were maintained at low values with rising temperatures. In particular, the Ag-CQDs dispersion exhibited better friction-minimizing performance at elevated temperatures, demonstrating that the carbon cores doped with silver played a part in the formation of the tribofilm and improved the carrying capacity due to the introduced silver element. Fig. 8 presents the 3D morphologies and 2D profiles of the alloys lubricated by PAO, PAO with 0.05 wt% CQDs/IL and PAO with 0.05 wt% Ag-CQDs/IL after 15-minute friction tests at different temperatures. It could be clearly seen that the worn track lubricated by PAO had a large width and depth at 200 °C and 300 °C (623.1 μm and 7.3 μm at 200 °C, 853.6 μm and 15.2 μm at 300 °C) due to the failed oil lubrication. In contrast, the worn track on the alloy plate lubricated by the CQDs and Ag-CQDs dispersions exhibited maximally reduced width and depth, to 403.9 μm and 6.3 μm and 333.4 μm and 3.1 μm at 200 °C and to 582.2 μm and 5.5 μm and 355.2 μm and 4.4 μm at 300 °C, respectively. All the width, depth and cross-section areas on the alloy plate lubricated by the CQDs and Ag-CQDs dispersions were much narrower and shallower compared with those on the alloy plate lubricated by the base oil at the same temperature. This demonstrated the excellent antiwear performance of these two additives. In practice, the worn track would be largely related to the plastic deformations frequently generated as a result of the indention and extrusion of the counterpart pair induced by pressure and shear [51]. Over the process of friction, the plastic strains exacerbated under local asperity contacts. This made the near-surface susceptible to shear, which resulted in a micro-cutting or ploughing force that would induce changes in the surface roughness. Hence, the increased plastic deformations would result in wider and deeper wear. The friction-induced deformations and subsequent roughness changes on the worn surfaces of the alloy would further aggravate the wear and cause the COF to increase.

The optical images of the upper Si3N4 balls sliding against GH4169 alloy with various lubricants at different temperatures were presented Fig. 9 . The PAO oil displayed severe wear morphologies with large wear scar diameters at 200 °C and 300 °C, respectively. In comparison with pure oil, the addition of CQDs and Ag-CQDs could effectively reduce the wear with relatively smooth worn surfaces and small wear scar diameters. These results illustrated that the PAO base oil featured the poor resistance to wear while the PAO with Ag-CQDs additive performed the best wear-resistance capacity among all of the samples at elevated temperatures. To better understand the effects of CQDs and Ag-CQDs on tribological performance, the worn surfaces of the alloy were characterized via SEM. Fig. 10 provides SEM images of the worn surfaces of the metal disk lubricated by different mediums after the friction tests at elevated temperatures. From Fig. 10a-b, it could be seen that the worn surfaces under pure PAO lubrication exhibited relatively obvious furrows and plastic deformations with some wear debris. Some delaminations and pits were also observed, indicating that severe wear occurred during the friction tests at elevated temperatures. This further suggested that the lubricating property of the base PAO oil oxidized as the temperature increased. In addition, some decomposition products, such as carbides and amorphous carbon, were generated, which might exacerbate the wear of the contact areas. The EDS results of marked areas showed that the content of the oxygen element increased due to elevated temperatures. From this, it could be inferred that tribo-oxidation reaction occurred on the worn surface, and an enamel layer was formed in the friction process. The reason for this was that defects were generated on the worn surface of the alloy in the friction process, and the oxygen atoms surrounding the surface of the alloy would easily diffuse into metals along the defects at elevated temperatures. The oxygen would react with the constituent elements of the alloy to produce an enamel layer with complex oxide phases. Generally, the enamel layer was hard and brittle in nature and exhibited good antiwear performance to protect the rubbing pair. However, the reciprocating shear would make the enamel layer prone to being removed while it was being rubbed against the ceramic materials that had higher hardness. This would weaken the role of the enamel layer in minimizing friction. As shown in Fig. 10c-f, the worn surfaces lubricated by the CQDs dispersion and Ag-CQDs dispersion appeared relatively smooth without obvious delamination, and only some small cracks and slight furrows could be observed. Furthermore, a layer covered the metal surface, which turned out to be helpful in alleviating the direct contact between the rubbing pair and preventing the contact surfaces from being damaged. Moreover, the EDS analysis results revealed the presence of sliver, nitrogen, some alloy components including nickel, iron and chromium, and high weight percentage of carbon and oxygen elements in the marked areas lubricated with the Ag-CQDs dispersion. This suggested that a layer composed of the tribo-oxidation products and Ag-CQDs formed on the worn surface. The lubricating property of the layer was mainly attributed to the presence of the Ag-CQDs, which could largely reduce the shear force. Meanwhile, the layer composed of alloy oxides and tribochemical products was less prone to being removed under a low shear force, which would in turn strengthen the layer as hard phases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the concentration on the tribological performance of Ag-CQDs additive under four-ball tests
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11 shows the mean COF and WSD values obtained when different concentrations of the Ag-CQDs additive were added in PAO oil under four-ball tests. It was clear that the added Ag-CQDs could promote the lubricating performance of the base oil and therefore significantly reduce the mean COFs and WSDs. Additionally, the COF values decreased with the Ag-CQDs concentration no greater than 0.05 wt%, and then slightly increased with the Ag-CQDs concentration greater than 0.05 wt%. The WSD values showed a similar trend with the COF values. Table 2 displays the surface roughness values of wear scars lubricated with oil containing varying concentrations of Ag-CQDs additives. Similar to the results of mean friction coefficient and WSD, the surface roughness values were decreased with the increasing concentration of the Ag-CQDs additives, and the minimum value was obtained when the concentration was 0.05 wt%. After that the roughness values began to increase as the concentration increased, but the values were still smaller than that of PAO and PAO with IL alone. These results revealed that there was an optimal concentration of the Ag-CQDs additive in terms of improving the antiwear performance of the base oil. This finding might be ascribed to the fact that an excessive amount of the Ag-CQDs additive tended to agglomerate on partial worn surfaces, which would block the sustained supply of the lubricating oil in the contact interface [52,53]. These experimental results also confirmed that a small amount of the Ag-CQDs additive could bring about remarkable improvements to the lubricating capacity of the PAO base oil and in turn enable significant drops in the COF and WSD under four-ball test conditions.

It is accepted that the loss of energy induced by the friction during the sliding process would be transformed into heat on the contact surface, which would increase the temperature of the friction pair and the lubricating medium [54]. Therefore, a smaller temperature variation would imply a smaller loss of energy under the same condition. Table 3 shows the temperature variations when different concentrations of Ag-CQDs were added in PAO oil under four-ball test conditions. Compared with the pure base oil, all mixtures of the base oil with different concentrations of the Ag-CQDs additive exhibited smaller temperature variations. In particular, the smallest temperature variation appeared when the concentration of the additive was 0.05 wt%. These results were similar with those obtained for the COF and WSD. This phenomenon could be explained that a proper concentration of the Ag-CQDs additive deposited on the wear scar could dissipate the heat with the assistance of the carbon materials with a high thermal conductivity, which would decrease the adhesive attraction between the rubbing pair and thus reduce the friction and wear [55].

To better explore the nanostructure of the tribofilm on the alloy’s worn surface lubricated with the Ag-CQDs dispersion at 300 °C, the FIB-TEM characterization was conducted. It could be seen that the triboflim was relatively uniform and dense on the worn surface, and the thickness was around 32.8 nm (Fig. 12 a and b). This tribofilm embedded with some nanocrystalline phases viewed as the friction-induced products could be observed between the Pt layer and superalloy substrate, which were probably composed of carbonates and some residual components from the organic solvent. The selected area electron diffraction patterns (SAED) showed clear diffraction spots for the Ag component, which could be ascribed to lattice plane (111). The SAED patterns also demonstrated the presence of metallic oxide phases in the alloy, as evidenced by the presence of diffraction spots (211), (220) and (311) from Cr2O3, NiO, and Fe2O3. The appearance of lattice plane (002) was assigned to the graphitic carbon. The EDX mapping results of the HAADF (High-Angle Annular Dark Field) area revealed that the tribofilm contained carbon, silver, oxygen, nitrogen and some alloy native elements (such as nickel, iron and chromium), illustrating that this compact layer consisted of metal oxides and embedded with the Ag-CQDs additive. This suggested that complex tribochemical reactions had occurred between the rubbing pair during the friction tests under the action of shear and heat. Therefore, it could be inferred that the compositions of the tribofilm included the metallic oxides of the alloy, some hard carbide particles, the added Ag-CQDs additive and their derivatives. These compositions could alleviate the direct contact and thus protect the sliding pair.

Fig. 13 shows the XPS spectrums of some typical elements on the worn track zone of the metal disk lubricated by PAO containing the Ag-CQDs additive at 300 °C. As illustrated in Fig. 13, the C1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three chemically shifted peaks at 284.5 eV, 286.0 eV and 288.5 eV. These peaks were attributed to the C-C/C = C, C-N/C-O, and C = O groups, suggesting that the Ag-CQDs deposited on the worn surface to play a dominant role in lubrication [56]. The O1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into three different peaks at 529.3 eV, 530.6 eV and 531.8 eV. They were attributed to the native complex oxides of the alloy as well as several oxygen-containing groups in the organic solvent [24]. The presence of an intensive peak at 368.5 eV was assigned to the silver component [31]. The N1s spectrum could be fitted into two different peaks centered at binding energies at 401.6 eV and 398.8 eV, which were associated with the metal nitride and organic nitrogen, indicating that the nitrogen was adsorbed on the worn surface [40]. The presence of Ni oxides was verified by the Ni2p peak at 853.9 eV, suggesting the tribo-oxidation had occurred during the friction process. The Fe2p peak emerged at 711.3 eV, which could be assigned to iron oxides [57]. The Cr2p intensive peak emerged at 476.4 eV, which was attributed to the Cr3+ in Cr2O3. From the surface characterization by XPS analysis, it could be known that the Ag-CQDs were adsorbed on the worn surface, which caused an adsorption film to be formed during the sliding process at high temperatures. This further resulted in some tribochemical products which were composed of metal oxides, organic and/or inorganic carbon, and organic nitrogen/metal nitride. Meanwhile, the tribochemical products generated a layer mixed with some alloy native oxides and introduced the Ag-CQDs additive and their derivatives, which deposited on the worn surface to enable alleviated direct contact between the rubbing pair.

Raman spectrometry was employed to further analyze the lubricating properties of PAO with the CQDs/IL additive and PAO with the Ag-CQDs/IL additive, as shown in Fig. 14 . According to the Raman spectrum of the worn surface lubricated by PAO with the CQDs/IL additive, the Raman characteristic peaks appeared around 1322 cm−1 (D band) and 1587 cm−1 (G band). This suggested that the stacked CQDs additive on the worn surface had transformed into a graphene-like structure of the carbon under the action of friction. Moreover, the relative intensity ratio of ID/IG increased as the temperature increased, indicating that the graphene-like structure of the stacked carbon shifted toward disordering with more defects [58]. This result revealed that the disordered carbon derived from the CQDs additive deposited on the rubbing surfaces. Additionally, the disorder and defect were lifted to higher levels as the temperature increased, which was beneficial to the lubricating performance at elevated temperatures. For the surface lubricated by the Ag-CQDs suspension, a band at 929 cm−1 could be seen besides D band and G band, which could be attributed to the complex silver compound. Similarly, the intensity ratios of D band and G band went up as the temperature increased, suggesting that more defects and wrinkles were generated under the action of shear and friction-induced heat. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) of the carbon derived from Ag-CQDs was higher than that of the carbon derived from CQDs at the same temperature. The possible reason for this could be explained that the introduced silver component could enhance the film-forming capacity of the carbon dots additive on the metal surface. Consequently, more defects and a more disordered structure would be produced after reciprocal shear. This shear-susceptible carbon formed a stable tribofilm on the worn surface, and caused the isolation of the straight asperity contact, thereby improving the lubricating performance.

Based on all the tests and characterization results described above, the lubricating mechanism of the Ag-CQDs suspension was proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 15 . During the friction process, when at relatively low temperatures, the worn surface of the superalloy would become positively charged since the low-energy electron was liable to be excited from the metal surface under the action of shear. The Ag-CQDs additive could quickly deposit and be embedded in the worn surface via physical absorption and electrostatic attraction to minimize the friction and repair the rubbing surfaces. Under the combined action of the pressure and friction-induced heat at elevated temperatures, complex tribochemical reactions would occur on the contact interface. The layer induced by tribochemical reaction, which was composed of metal native oxides, carbides, nitrides, silver compound and their derivatives, could increasingly form on the worn surface. The stacked carbon derived from Ag-CQDs produced more defects and a more disordered structure under the action of shear to form a graphene-like structure, and endowed the protective layer with lubricating capacity at elevated temperatures. Meanwhile, the metal oxides, nitrides and some amorphous carbides as hard phases enhanced strength and carrying capacity for the tribofilm. Therefore, it was reasonable to infer that the coverage of the tribofilm could minimize the friction between the rubbing surfaces by alleviating the direct contact between them.

The Ag-CQDs compound as lubricating oil additive was successfully synthesized using a hydrothermal method, and the dispersion stability in PAO base oil was improved via the introduction of IL assisted by ultrasound vibration. The tribological behaviors and lubricating mechanism of the functionalized CQDs and Ag-CQDs additives were further explored. The conclusions could be summarized as follows:
1)
The CQDs and Ag-CQDs could be synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method, and the as-prepared particles with narrow size distribution could be well dispersed in PAO under the combination of IL modification and ultrasonic treatment.
2)
Both the CQDs and Ag-CQDs additives could significantly enhance the lubricating performance of PAO oil under four-ball tests. Due to their ultrasmall sizes, the CQDs and Ag-CQDs additives could enter the damaged zone to realize mending during the friction. Comparatively, the Ag-CQDs additive performed better in friction reduction than the CQDs additive because of the introduction of Ag component, which contributed to improving carrying capacity for the lubricants.
3)
The CQDs and Ag-CQDs dispersions exhibited slightly lower COFs than that of the base oil at relatively low temperatures under the reciprocal sliding configuration. As the temperature increased, the Ag-CQDs dispersion exhibited lower COFs and smaller wear volume than both the base oil and the CQDs dispersion. At elevated temperatures, under the action of pressure and friction-induced heat, the Ag-CQDs deposited on the worn surface, which caused the formation of a tribofilm that contained metal oxides, complex carbides and nitrides. This could effectively alleviate the direct contact between the rubbing surfaces and prevent the contact interface from being damaged.

Junhai Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xinran Li: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yuanyuan Deng: Investigation, Formal analysis. Shihao Chen: Investigation, Formal analysis. Wenfeng Liang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Lixiu Zhang: Resources. Xiaoyi Wei: Formal analysis.
